reviews.reasoning: The comment in the declaration explicitly references sourcemap usage and pairing of sources with sourcesContent. At runtime, callers will pass source filenames and source content into the mapping structures that this SetArray type helps maintain. If build tooling or library consumers attach full source contents (which may include secrets, API keys, or private code) and then emit or publish generated source maps, those secrets will be exposed to anyone with access to the sourcemap (browsers, public repositories, or upload endpoints). The .d.ts documents that this helper is intended exactly for the arrays that carry source content, making the risk directly relevant.
reviews.mitigation: Treat source content as sensitive by default. Add size and content filters on inputs (reject extremely large sources; optionally redact or omit sourcesContent when generating public sourcemaps). Provide configuration flags to omit sourcesContent from emitted maps, and document the risks prominently. Also validate/scan sourceContent for patterns that might indicate secrets before including them in published artifacts.

reviews.issue: Unbounded resource consumption from unvalidated/large inputs used in SetArray-managed arrays
reviews.reasoning: The API exposes operations to add keys to an internal array and maintain indexes. If callers (or attackers) provide extremely large numbers of unique keys or very large sourceContent payloads (as described in the RELEVANT_CONTEXT), the runtime implementation that uses these types can consume excessive memory or CPU while building or serializing mapping arrays, leading to denial-of-service or OOM conditions. The declaration indicates arrays and manipulators exist but does not impose limits; the runtime must therefore handle untrusted, potentially large inputs safely.
reviews.mitigation: Enforce sane upper bounds in the runtime implementation for the number of unique entries and for the size of associated content (e.g., max number of sources, max total bytes for sourcesContent). Stream or chunk large payloads where possible, and fail fast with clear errors for inputs exceeding configured quotas. Validate input sizes before inserting into SetArray and document default limits and configuration knobs.

reviews.issue: Embedding original source text via sourcesContent can leak sensitive data
reviews.code_snippet: sourcesContent?: readonly (string | null)[];
reviews.reasoning: The declaration exposes sourcesContent as an optional array of source text strings. At runtime the corresponding API will embed whatever strings are provided into the produced source map (sourcesContent). If callers supply original source code that contains secrets (API keys, credentials, internal configuration, or comments), those secrets will be included in the emitted map and can be exposed to any client or third‑party that receives the source map. The RELEVANT_CONTEXT explicitly warns that "embedding original source code via sourcesContent will include whatever strings are provided" and that publishing maps with embedded sources may disclose secrets.
reviews.mitigation: Do not embed full original sources unless necessary. Before adding sourcesContent, scan and redact secrets and sensitive tokens from source text. Provide configuration options to disable embedding sourcesContent by default and require an explicit opt-in. Document the risk in runtime docs and add tests that ensure sensitive patterns (API keys, private config) are not present when sourcesContent is enabled.

reviews.issue: sourceRoot, file, and sources fields can expose internal file paths or external URLs
reviews.reasoning: The types allow arbitrary strings for file, sourceRoot and each entry in sources. These values may contain absolute filesystem paths, repository paths, internal hostnames, or external URLs. If a sourceRoot or a source entry is a URL, tools or humans might fetch those locations or infer infrastructure details. The RELEVANT_CONTEXT calls out that "source file names and sourceRoot can expose internal filesystem paths, repository structure, or internal hostnames/URLs" and that sourceRoot and source names may be URLs.
reviews.mitigation: Normalize and validate file, sourceRoot and sources at runtime. Strip absolute filesystem prefixes or convert to relative canonical paths before emitting maps. Disallow or validate remote URLs (require only file paths or whitelist domains). Provide an option to mask or rewrite source filenames for public builds (e.g., map internal paths to sanitized identifiers).

reviews.issue: Unbounded mappings or very large sourcesContent can cause resource exhaustion (DoS)
reviews.reasoning: The interfaces permit mappings to be arbitrarily large (either as an encoded VLQ string or as nested arrays of SourceMapSegment) and sourcesContent to contain arbitrarily large strings. At runtime, constructing or encoding very large mapping structures or embedding very large source texts can consume large amounts of memory and CPU, potentially causing process OOMs or high CPU usage. The RELEVANT_CONTEXT notes resource exhaustion/DoS as a relevant risk and that callers control the number and size of entries.
reviews.mitigation: Enforce size limits and quotas in the runtime implementation: maximum total mappings, maximum mapping string length, maximum total bytes for all sourcesContent, and per‑source size limits. Stream processing for large inputs and fail fast with clear errors when limits are exceeded. Document limits and provide configuration for trusted vs. untrusted callers.

reviews.issue: String fields that may be inlined or rendered (expression, names, mapping.name) can lead to XSS if embedded into HTML/JS without escaping
reviews.reasoning: The declaration contains multiple freeform string properties (expression, mapping.name, names entries) that at runtime can contain user‑controlled content. The RELEVANT_CONTEXT warns that if a generated source map is embedded into an HTML page or concatenated into scripts without proper escaping, untrusted content in sourcesContent, names, or expressions could contribute to XSS or broken HTML/JS. While the .d.ts cannot perform escaping itself, it documents the API surface that allows such strings to be supplied and later inlined by tooling or web pages.
reviews.mitigation: Treat all string fields that may be emitted into HTML or JS as untrusted. Ensure the runtime code escapes or encodes these strings appropriately before embedding into HTML or script contexts. Prefer serving source maps as separate files with correct Content‑Type and not inlined into HTML. Document this requirement for consumers and add tests that ensure escaping behavior when maps are embedded.

reviews.issue: Permissive declaration types can cause a false sense of security if runtime behavior diverges from the .d.ts
reviews.reasoning: Declaration files provide compile‑time guarantees only. The RELEVANT_CONTEXT emphasizes that a mismatch between declarations and runtime behavior is a security risk because developers may assume the type system enforces guarantees (e.g., non‑null, sanitized values) which are not enforced at runtime. The types here are permissive (many plain strings and optional/null unions), which can lead callers to rely on compile‑time validation rather than implementing runtime checks.
reviews.mitigation: Document the runtime validation requirements clearly in the package README and API docs. Implement defensive runtime checks and validation for all inputs that originate from untrusted sources (e.g., HTTP payloads, user uploads). Provide runtime helper functions that perform safe normalization/validation (and expose types that represent validated/branded values if appropriate). Add tests to ensure the runtime rejects or sanitizes invalid or dangerous inputs regardless of TypeScript usage.

reviews.issue: Source contents and source paths are stored and returned verbatim, enabling sensitive information disclosure.
reviews.reasoning: The code stores raw source identifiers and the full source text in _sources and _sourcesContent via setSourceContent and then returns them verbatim in toDecodedMap. RELEVANT_CONTEXT states these fields may contain file-system paths and full source text and that these values are externally controlled (consumer-provided). If a GenMapping instance is populated from untrusted input, exposing sources, sourceRoot, file, or sourcesContent in the returned map can leak internal file paths, implementation details, or secrets embedded in source comments/strings. There is no sanitization, redaction, or size limits applied before inclusion in the returned object.
reviews.mitigation: Treat source paths and sourcesContent as untrusted. Apply policy-based sanitization or redaction before returning or persisting maps that will be exposed. Options: (1) Do not include sourcesContent for untrusted inputs (omit or replace with null), (2) normalize or strip absolute filesystem paths from source names and sourceRoot, (3) enforce a maximum length for sourcesContent and truncate with an audit/log, (4) provide an explicit calling API flag to opt-in to embedding source content.

reviews.issue: Unbounded array growth when creating per-line mappings can lead to memory exhaustion (Denial of Service).
reviews.reasoning: getIndex allocates array entries up to the supplied index (genLine). RELEVANT_CONTEXT explains genLine/segments come from decoded source maps and are externally controlled. If callers pass a very large genLine value (or malicious source map contains very large line indices), getIndex will allocate many empty arrays, consuming large amounts of memory and enabling resource exhaustion. There are no bounds checks or caps on genLine before growth, and addSegmentInternal accepts genLine directly from caller input (no validation).
reviews.mitigation: Validate and bound genLine before calling getIndex or inside getIndex. Enforce a configurable maximum number of lines and fail-fast (throw or skip) when exceeded. Additionally, validate genColumn and other positional inputs for reasonable ranges. Consider streaming processing or backpressure to avoid accumulating huge in-memory structures for untrusted input.

reviews.issue: No runtime validation of numeric position inputs; the assert helper is a no-op, allowing malformed indices to be accepted.
reviews.reasoning: The code uses a TypeScript-only assert helper that is a runtime no-op. RELEVANT_CONTEXT notes this library accepts externally controlled mapping entries (sourceLine, sourceColumn, genLine, genColumn). Because assert does not validate at runtime, callers can pass negative, NaN, Infinity, or extremely large numeric values. Those can lead to invalid map contents or trigger the unbounded allocation and other errors elsewhere. There are no explicit checks (isFinite, >= 0, integer) for these position parameters in addSegmentInternal or addMappingInternal.
reviews.mitigation: Replace the no-op assert with runtime validation checks: verify values are integers, finite, and >= 0 and within expected maximums. Return or throw clear errors on invalid input. Consider defensive programming in public API surface (addMapping/addSegment) to reject malformed mapping entries before they mutate internal state.

reviews.issue: The cast helper and use of 'as any' bypass TypeScript access controls, enabling external code to inspect or manipulate private internals.
reviews.reasoning: The cast function returns the passed object as any and the module uses it to access fields declared private on GenMapping. RELEVANT_CONTEXT explicitly calls out that this bypasses TypeScript privacy. If a malicious or mutated GenMapping instance is provided by callers (or if external code has access to the object and changes its internals), functions here (e.g., allMappings, toDecodedMap) will operate on and expose those internals. This weakens encapsulation and increases the risk that sensitive fields are read or mutated unexpectedly.
reviews.mitigation: Avoid 'as any' casts that expose private internals across trust boundaries. Use explicit public accessor methods on GenMapping for required operations (e.g., getters for names/sources/mappings) and validate the map's shape at the API boundary. If performance is a concern, limit the exposed surface to minimal, well-documented read-only accessors rather than blanket 'any' casts.

reviews.issue: Unsanitized names, source paths and source contents are returned and could lead to injection or XSS when the resulting map is embedded in HTML or logs.
reviews.reasoning: toDecodedMap returns arrays of names, sources and sourcesContent with no escaping or sanitization. RELEVANT_CONTEXT notes that these values are externally controlled and may be later embedded into HTML (e.g., devtools pages), logs, or other sinks. If consumers take the resulting map and render values in an HTML context (or write to logs/console that an attacker can view), these unsanitized strings could enable DOM-based XSS or other injection attacks. The library itself does not perform any contextual encoding.
reviews.mitigation: Document that consumers must treat map fields as untrusted and perform context-appropriate escaping before embedding them into HTML, attributes, or shell commands. Consider providing helper APIs to produce safe HTML-rendered previews (escaped) or to offer an option to automatically escape or strip suspicious characters when generating maps intended for untrusted consumption. Enforce size limits and optional content stripping for sourcesContent to reduce attack surface.

reviews.issue: Embedding original source text via sourcesContent can leak sensitive data
reviews.code_snippet: sourcesContent?: readonly (string | null)[];
reviews.reasoning: The SourceMapV3 type exposes a sourcesContent field which holds the original source text for each source file. At runtime, values conforming to this type are often written into distributed .map files or sent to tooling. Including full original sources in sourcemaps can leak secrets, credentials, or proprietary source code to consumers of the map (browsers, CDNs, third-party tooling). TypeScript types are compile-time only and do not prevent runtime code from populating this field with sensitive data.
reviews.mitigation: Do not include sourcesContent in publicly distributed sourcemaps by default. Make inclusion opt-in and document the risk. Enforce server-side/packaging-time checks that strip or redact sourcesContent unless explicitly allowed. Apply size limits to each content entry and scan contents for high-risk patterns (API keys, private tokens) before embedding.

reviews.issue: file and sourceRoot fields can be used for path/URL injection or confusing links
reviews.reasoning: file and sourceRoot are string fields that are typically used to construct file-system paths or URLs by downstream tooling or UI. If attacker-controlled values are placed here they can cause path traversal, injection of external URLs, or lead consumers to fetch attacker-controlled resources (e.g., malicious sourceRoot pointing at an external host). The type definitions do not enforce any normalization or allowed schemes.
reviews.mitigation: Normalize and validate file and sourceRoot at runtime. Reject or sanitize values containing path traversal (..), disallow unsafe schemes (javascript:, data:), and restrict to a whitelist of allowed hosts or relative paths when building links. Prefer using canonicalized, well-formed URLs or controlled mapping tables rather than raw user-provided strings.

reviews.issue: Encoded/decoded mappings can be arbitrarily large and cause resource exhaustion (DoS)
reviews.reasoning: The EncodedSourceMap exposes a mappings string and DecodedSourceMap exposes mappings as nested arrays. A malicious or malformed mappings payload (very large string or extremely large nested arrays) can cause excessive CPU and memory usage when parsing, encoding, decoding, or manipulating the map, leading to DoS in build tooling, servers, or clients. Type definitions do not enforce size limits.
reviews.mitigation: Enforce input size limits on mappings (max length of encoded string, max number of decoded segments and lines). Use streaming or incremental processing for very large maps. Validate and reject inputs that exceed safe thresholds. Add unit tests that assert behavior on boundary/large inputs.

reviews.issue: Position (Pos) fields are unbounded numbers; invalid/out-of-range positions can cause logic errors or crashes
reviews.reasoning: Pos allows any number for line and column. Consumers will typically assume 1-based line and 0-based column and may index arrays or perform arithmetic based on these values. Negative, non-integer, NaN, or extremely large values (or zero for line) could cause out-of-bounds array accesses, exceptions, or incorrect mappings. Because these are only compile-time types, invalid runtime values can flow through unchecked.
reviews.mitigation: Validate Pos objects at runtime: ensure line is a positive integer (>=1), column is an integer (>=0), and both are within expected bounds for the source. Normalize or clamp inputs where appropriate and fail-fast with clear errors for malformed positions.

reviews.issue: String fields used by mappings (name, source, BindingExpressionRange.expression) may be rendered into UIs and cause XSS if not escaped
reviews.reasoning: Fields such as expression, source and name are arbitrary strings that may be displayed in web-based developer tools, UIs, or injected into HTML templates. If values are attacker-controlled and are inserted into DOM sinks without escaping, this can lead to DOM-based XSS. The type system does not provide output-encoding guarantees.
reviews.mitigation: Treat all string fields as untrusted when used in HTML contexts. Escape or HTML-encode values before inserting into DOM, avoid using innerHTML, and prefer safe DOM APIs (textContent). When building links from source/name, validate and canonicalize values to prevent injecting active content.

reviews.issue: Types are compile-time only and provide no runtime validation; unsafe assumptions can be exploited
reviews.reasoning: This file is purely type-level (uses 'import type' and only exports interfaces/types). TypeScript's compile-time checks do not exist at runtime; therefore, code that assumes objects strictly conforming to these types can be passed malicious or malformed data. Without runtime schema validation, callers and consumers can encounter unexpected values leading to logic errors, crashes, or security issues (e.g., indexing errors, injection of unexpected strings into UIs).
reviews.mitigation: Add explicit runtime validation for external inputs that claim to match these types. Use a runtime schema validator (e.g., zod, io-ts, yup) or hand-written checks at API boundaries that enforce type shape, ranges, allowed string formats, and size limits. Clearly document which fields are treated as untrusted.

reviews.issue: Potential exposure of original source contents via sourcesContent when excludeContent is not set
reviews.reasoning: The function normalizes options into an opts object and then constructs a SourceMap using that opts. If callers pass an Options object (or leave excludeContent unset/false), the resulting SourceMap may include embedded sourcesContent from input maps or content provided by the loader. The RELEVANT_CONTEXT states that sourcesContent (and ctx.content provided by the loader) will be included unless excludeContent is true, which can leak sensitive source code if the inputs or loader are untrusted. The file-level evidence shows where opts is created and passed to the SourceMap constructor, demonstrating control over whether content is included in the output.
reviews.mitigation: Force excludeContent: true when processing untrusted input, or explicitly validate and sanitize any sourcesContent before including it. Document and enforce a policy for callers that disallows embedding sourcesContent from untrusted sources. If the library is used in server-side contexts that accept external maps, consider exposing a wrapper that always sets excludeContent true and/or strips sourcesContent before calling remapping.

reviews.issue: Untrusted loader callback is executed and may perform arbitrary I/O or return malicious/large sourcemaps
reviews.reasoning: The loader argument is provided by the caller and is passed directly into buildSourceMapTree. The RELEVANT_CONTEXT explicitly warns that the loader will be called for each encountered source and may perform file I/O, network requests, or other side effects. Because remapping invokes buildSourceMapTree with the supplied loader, a malicious or poorly implemented loader can cause arbitrary file reads, network requests, excessive work, or injection of crafted sourcemaps into the remapping process. The file shows where the loader is accepted and forwarded into the tree-building step.
reviews.mitigation: Treat loader code as privileged: require callers to supply safe loader implementations, or provide/encourage use of a secure default loader that enforces sandboxing, path allow-lists, timeouts, and size limits. Document loader responsibilities and advise caching of results to avoid repeated expensive calls. In contexts accepting loaders from third parties, run them in a sandbox or restrict available APIs.

reviews.issue: Enabling decodedMappings can increase memory/CPU usage and enable resource-exhaustion DoS
reviews.code_snippet:
const opts =
    typeof options === 'object' ? options : { excludeContent: !!options, decodedMappings: false };
reviews.reasoning: The normalized opts object includes decodedMappings as an option. The RELEVANT_CONTEXT warns that decodedMappings mode will produce decoded mapping arrays instead of compact VLQ-encoded strings, increasing memory usage and processing cost. Since options are externally controlled, an attacker could request decoded mappings or supply maps that cause excessively large decoded structures, leading to high memory consumption or service degradation. The file's opts normalization is the evidence that callers can enable decodedMappings.
reviews.mitigation: Limit or disallow decodedMappings for untrusted inputs; enforce maximum allowed mapping count/size when decodedMappings is requested. Provide server-side guards such as caps on decoded mapping array sizes, timeouts for trace operations, and early rejection for overly large or deeply nested maps. Consider exposing a separate API for decoded outputs that performs stricter validation.

reviews.issue: Deeply nested or large source-map chains can cause excessive recursion and resource consumption during tree building and tracing
reviews.code_snippet:
const tree = buildSourceMapTree(input, loader);
  return new SourceMap(traceMappings(tree), opts);
reviews.reasoning: The implementation builds a source-map tree from the provided input(s) and then traces mappings through that tree. The RELEVANT_CONTEXT highlights that buildSourceMapTree walks nested sourcemaps by calling the loader recursively; extremely deep or cyclic chains, or very many chained sourcemaps, can cause many loader invocations, high CPU work, deep recursion, and large memory usage when traceMappings runs. The file shows both the tree construction and the tracing/SourceMap construction, which are the places resource exhaustion can occur.
reviews.mitigation: Enforce limits on maximum traversal depth and total number of loader invocations. Detect and break cycles in the chain. Apply quotas on total mappings and sources processed and fail fast when limits are exceeded. When used in shared/server contexts, run remapping operations under time and memory limits.

reviews.issue: Unbounded recursion and resource exhaustion when tracing nested source maps
reviews.reasoning: The recursive function originalPositionFor unconditionally recurses into child source maps by calling itself with source.sources[segment[1]] without any depth check or global limits. The traceMappings function iterates over all decoded mappings without applying size/cost limits, so a malicious or pathological chain of maps (deep nesting or very large mapping tables) can cause deep recursion, excessive CPU, and memory consumption (DoS). The RELEVANT_CONTEXT explicitly calls out DoS/resource exhaustion risks from deeply nested or extremely large mappings and recommends depth/size limits.
reviews.mitigation: Enforce explicit limits: add a recursion depth parameter and refuse to recurse beyond a configurable maximum depth. Cap the total number of segments/mappings processed (and bail out with a clear error if exceeded). Consider iterative traversal instead of recursion or use trampolining to avoid stack overflows. Apply timeouts or work quotas when processing large maps and validate map sizes before heavy processing.

reviews.issue: Potential sensitive content exposure by embedding unvalidated source content into output map
reviews.reasoning: The code takes source content returned by Source / OriginalSource nodes and unconditionally calls setSourceContent(gen, source, content) when content is non-null. The RELEVANT_CONTEXT warns that sourcesContent may contain secrets and that embedding source text can leak sensitive data. There is no size check or policy applied here to prevent embedding large or sensitive content into the generated map.
reviews.mitigation: Validate and bound the size of source content before embedding (e.g., maximum bytes). Add an option that enforces exclusion of sourcesContent (and respect it here), or require upstream validation of ctx.content. Explicitly sanitize or redact content known to contain secrets. Log and fail or omit embedding when content is excessively large or when the caller's policy forbids embedding sources.

reviews.issue: Unchecked index access of mapping segment indices may cause crashes or incorrect behavior
reviews.reasoning: The code indexes into rootSources, rootNames and later into source.sources using values from segment (e.g., segment[1], segment[4]) without validating that those indices are within bounds or that the retrieved values are defined. Malformed or attacker-controlled sourcemaps could contain segment index values outside the arrays' bounds, causing undefined accesses or runtime exceptions (uncaught errors) that can crash the process or terminate processing prematurely (DoS). The RELEVANT_CONTEXT explicitly calls sourcemaps externally-controlled and may be malformed.
reviews.mitigation: Validate segment index values before using them: check that segment[1] is a valid integer within rootSources.length, that segment[4] (if present) is within rootNames.length, and that source.sources[segment[1]] exists before recursing. On invalid indices, handle gracefully (skip entry, emit a warning, or abort with a clear error). Consider schema validation for incoming maps before processing.

reviews.issue: Bypassing TypeScript type-safety with 'as any' in Source() can mask unsafe shapes from callers
reviews.reasoning: The Source factory casts the returned object to 'any' and then to 'Sources', which suppresses TypeScript's structural checks. This makes it easy for invalid or malformed objects to be created without compile-time errors, increasing the risk that runtime assumptions (e.g., presence of expected properties or correct discriminants between MapSource and OriginalSource) are violated. While not directly executable code injection, this weakens safety checks and can hide bugs that lead to incorrect processing of untrusted input.
reviews.mitigation: Return a properly typed discriminated union without using 'as any'. Use explicit discriminant fields (e.g., a 'type' property) and ensure the constructors produce values that satisfy the Sources union. Add runtime assertions in development builds to validate the expected shape of created nodes.

reviews.issue: Loader callback exposes an IO surface that can be used to read arbitrary files or network resources based on SourceMap 'sources' entries.
reviews.reasoning: The SourceMapLoader type shows that callers provide a loader function that receives a file path (file: string) originating from source map 'sources' entries and a LoaderContext. The loader's return type is a SourceMapInput or null/undefined, which implies the loader will generally resolve the file path and supply a source map or content. As noted in the provided RELEVANT_CONTEXT, typical loader implementations perform filesystem or network IO to resolve these file strings. Because the file argument is derived from externally supplied source maps, an attacker who controls those maps could cause a loader that does no path validation to read arbitrary files (including absolute paths or path traversal), expose secrets, or perform unintended network requests.
reviews.mitigation: Treat all 'file' arguments as untrusted. Implement strict path validation and resolution rules in the loader: restrict resolution to an allow-list of directories or a controlled virtual filesystem, reject absolute paths and paths containing '..' unless explicitly permitted and normalized, and disallow automatic network fetching unless explicitly required and authorized. Log and audit loader accesses. Prefer running loaders in a sandboxed environment with least privilege for IO.

reviews.issue: Loader can override ctx.content, enabling embedding of arbitrary file contents into generated sourcemaps (possible sensitive data leakage).
reviews.reasoning: The LoaderContext type includes a mutable 'content' property (content: string | null | undefined) which loaders may set or override. As described in RELEVANT_CONTEXT, remapping may include sourcesContent in the output source map. If a loader sets ctx.content to the contents of files resolved from untrusted source map entries, that content can end up embedded in the output map and potentially exposed to consumers (e.g., uploaded artifacts, developer tools, or public artifacts). This creates an information exposure risk when the loader embeds secrets, credentials, or other sensitive files.
reviews.mitigation: Default to excluding sourcesContent unless explicitly requested. Enforce policies in the loader to redact or omit content for sensitive files (e.g., files outside a project root or with filenames that match secret patterns). Provide an explicit opt-in mechanism and review that behavior in CI/CD processes. When embedding content is necessary, sanitize/strip secrets and limit content size.

reviews.issue: No type-level limit on remapping recursion depth; LoaderContext.depth allows arbitrarily deep traversal, risking resource exhaustion or stack overflows.
reviews.reasoning: The LoaderContext exposes 'depth: number' to indicate traversal depth, but the types and this file do not enforce any maximum depth. As RELEVANT_CONTEXT explains, map graphs may be deeply nested or cyclic. Without enforcing a maximum depth or cycle detection in the implementation that uses these types, the remapping process can perform deep recursion or repeated loads leading to high CPU/memory use or stack overflows (Denial of Service). The presence of depth without an accompanying limit in types indicates the API surface leaves it to the loader or remapping implementation to enforce controls.
reviews.mitigation: Enforce a maximum traversal depth in the remapping implementation and/or require loader implementations to respect and check ctx.depth before performing further lookups. Add cycle detection (cache visited source identifiers) to avoid revisiting the same map. Fail gracefully with an informative error when depth limits are exceeded.

reviews.issue: Options allow inclusion of sourcesContent via excludeContent flag, which can inadvertently enable embedding of large or sensitive content.
reviews.reasoning: The Options type exposes 'excludeContent?: boolean'. If code using these types sets excludeContent to false (or omits it and the implementation defaults to including content), the remapping flow may include full source contents in the resulting map. As noted in RELEVANT_CONTEXT, including sourcesContent can leak secrets or produce very large artifacts, contributing to information exposure and resource concerns. The type shows the API permits toggling this behavior, which is a security-relevant configuration.
reviews.mitigation: Default to excludeContent: true in implementations (i.e., do not embed source content unless explicitly requested). Document the implications of enabling sourcesContent and add size limits and content filters when embedding is enabled. Require explicit opt-in with a conspicuous API or configuration flag rather than a simple boolean defaulting to unsafe behavior.

reviews.issue: Decoding/encoding untrusted mapping input can cause resource exhaustion (DoS)
reviews.reasoning: The constructor immediately passes the externally-supplied `map` into toDecodedMap/toEncodedMap without validating size or complexity. Per RELEVANT_CONTEXT, the encoder/decoder transforms encoded VLQ mapping strings into decoded segment arrays; decoding large or maliciously crafted mappings can require large CPU and memory (heavy VLQ decoding, large arrays). Because this file offers no guards (size limits, depth limits, or early rejection) the decode/encode operation is a direct resource-exhaustion attack surface when handling untrusted input.
reviews.mitigation: Validate and bound the input map before decoding: enforce maximum mapping string length, maximum number of sources/sourcesContent size, and maximum nesting depth for sectioned maps. Consider rejecting maps that exceed configured thresholds. Run decode/encode on untrusted input inside a time- or memory-limited worker/sandbox, catch and handle exceptions from the codec, and fail fast on obviously malformed inputs.

reviews.issue: toString() serializes the entire SourceMap object including sourcesContent, risking sensitive data leakage
reviews.reasoning: The class stores `sourcesContent` when options.excludeContent is false, taking potentially large and sensitive source file contents from the input map (RELEVANT_CONTEXT notes that sourcesContent entries are externally-controlled). The toString() implementation unconditionally JSON.stringify(this), which will include sourcesContent if present. This can inadvertently expose original source text or other sensitive data when the object is logged, emitted, or otherwise serialized, especially when remapping untrusted or private inputs.
reviews.mitigation: Avoid serializing sensitive fields by default. Either: (a) make toString omit sourcesContent and other sensitive fields, or (b) provide a toSafeString(excludeContent?: boolean) that respects excludeContent and redacts or omits large/sensitive fields. Document that toString may expose embedded sources and recommend using the excludeContent option for untrusted inputs. Additionally, enforce an option and default to excludeContent=true in high-risk contexts.

reviews.issue: Blind TypeScript 'as' casts bypass runtime validation of untrusted map fields
reviews.reasoning: The code uses TypeScript `as` casts to assign fields from `out` into the SourceMap instance without validating the runtime shape or types of those fields. The GenMapping input and the resulting `out` object come from external sources (RELEVANT_CONTEXT). Type assertions only affect compile-time checking and do not perform runtime validation; malformed or intentionally crafted maps could have unexpected types or structures, leading to runtime exceptions, incorrect mapping behavior, or later code paths operating on invalid assumptions. This weakens type-safety guarantees at the trust boundary.
reviews.mitigation: Introduce explicit runtime validation for the expected fields on `out` before assignment: check that mappings is the expected string/array shape, names is an array of strings, sources is an array, and sourcesContent (if present) matches expected length/type. Use a schema validator (e.g., zod, io-ts) or manual checks and fail early with clear errors for invalid maps. Avoid relying solely on `as` casts when processing untrusted inputs.

reviews.issue: Externally-supplied loader can be used to perform arbitrary filesystem or network access (SSRF / path traversal)
reviews.reasoning: The code constructs a LoaderContext from data derived directly from the parsed TraceMap (sourceFile) and immediately invokes the externally-supplied loader with ctx.source and ctx. The loader callback is fully responsible for fetching/returning a nested sourcemap or source content. Because the source value comes from untrusted source-map inputs (see RELEVANT_CONTEXT) there is no validation or sanitization of the path/URL before handing it to the loader. A malicious or misconfigured loader or input could therefore cause arbitrary file reads (path traversal), network requests (SSRF), or other side effects depending on the loader implementation, leading to sensitive data exposure or remote requests executed in the application's privilege context.
reviews.mitigation: Validate and sanitize ctx.source before calling the loader. Enforce an allowlist of allowed schemes/hosts and restrict filesystem access to an allowed root; normalize paths to prevent '../' traversal. Make the loader a least-privileged operation (e.g., deny network access unless explicitly allowed). Consider passing a sanitized or resolved path/URL to the loader rather than raw values from the map and include explicit caller-provided policies controlling remote fetches.

reviews.issue: No cycle detection for chained sourcemaps — infinite recursion / resource exhaustion possible
reviews.code_snippet:
// If there is a sourcemap, then we need to recurse into it to load its source files.
    if (sourceMap) return build(new TraceMap(sourceMap, source), loader, source, depth);
reviews.reasoning: When loader returns a sourceMap, the code immediately constructs a new TraceMap and recurses into build() for that map. There is no tracking of previously-visited maps or sources, so a set of sourcemaps that reference each other (A → B → A) or a cycle introduced via crafted maps can cause unbounded recursion and eventually a stack overflow or memory exhaustion. RELEVANT_CONTEXT explicitly warns that sourcemaps could reference each other, and this code does not implement any visited-set or cycle-breaker.
reviews.mitigation: Maintain a visited set or map keyed by a stable identifier (resolved source path + map content hash) and check it before recursing. If a cycle is detected, break the recursion and either treat the node as an original source or surface a controlled error. Also enforce an overall node or recursion limit to cap resource usage.

reviews.issue: No maximum recursion depth enforcement — deep or malicious chains can cause stack overflow / DoS
reviews.code_snippet: const depth = importerDepth + 1;
reviews.reasoning: The code increments importerDepth for each recursive call and uses depth in subsequent loader contexts, but there is no maximum depth check anywhere in this file. If an attacker supplies a long chain of nested sourcemaps (or crafts maps to force many recursions), this will increment depth indefinitely and may cause stack overflow or severe resource consumption. RELEVANT_CONTEXT highlights that depth is externally influenced and recommends enforcing limits; the implementation here lacks such checks.
reviews.mitigation: Introduce a configurable maximum depth and validate importerDepth/depth before recursing or before calling the loader. If the limit is reached, return a controlled error or stop recursion, optionally marking the node as unresolved. Log and surface an actionable error rather than allowing a crash.

reviews.issue: Uncaught parsing/constructor errors when creating TraceMap from untrusted input can crash the process
reviews.reasoning: TraceMap is constructed directly from input values (both initial input and loader-returned sourceMap) without try/catch or validation. The TraceMap constructor likely parses sourcemap JSON and may throw on malformed inputs or unexpected types. Because exceptions are not caught here, a single malformed or intentionally crafted sourcemap can throw and potentially crash the caller's process. RELEVANT_CONTEXT notes that TraceMap/build can throw on malformed input and recommends graceful handling.
reviews.mitigation: Validate sizes and types of incoming map blobs before constructing TraceMap. Wrap TraceMap construction and parsing in try/catch blocks and surface controlled errors to the caller rather than letting exceptions propagate. Consider rejecting overly-large or malformed inputs early and computing a safe maximum allowed map size.

reviews.issue: No limits on sourcesContent retention — large embedded source contents can exhaust memory
reviews.reasoning: The code accepts sourcesContent provided by the parsed TraceMap or the loader and stores it in the returned OriginalSource nodes without any size checks or caps. If an attacker embeds extremely large source contents in sourcemaps (or the loader returns huge contents), building the tree will allocate and retain these blobs in memory, potentially causing out-of-memory conditions. RELEVANT_CONTEXT warns about resource exhaustion via sourcesContent.
reviews.mitigation: Enforce a configurable maximum size per sourceContent and a total memory budget for stored contents. Consider streaming large contents instead of retaining them in-memory, or omitting sourcesContent by default unless explicitly requested. Validate and possibly truncate or reject content exceeding thresholds.

reviews.issue: Synchronous loader invocation can block the event loop and enable availability DoS
reviews.reasoning: The loader is invoked synchronously on the hot path. If a loader implementation performs slow I/O (disk reads, network fetches) synchronously, it will block the Node.js event loop during the call, possibly causing request latency spikes or enabling DoS by intentionally slowing loader responses. The file's own TODO notes lack of async support; that implies synchronous blocking is by-design, but it is a risk for availability.
reviews.mitigation: Make the loader asynchronous (Promise-based) and convert the build process to support async loading, or require and document that provided loaders must be non-blocking. Additionally, enforce timeouts on loader operations and run parsing/IO in worker threads where blocking is acceptable.

reviews.issue: Unvalidated resolved URI can lead to SSRF or open redirect when result is used for network requests or redirects.
reviews.reasoning: The declaration shows the function accepts externally controlled strings (input and optional base) and returns an unresolved string. RELEVANT_CONTEXT explicitly states both parameters are externally controlled and that the resolved URI may be used for redirects or outgoing requests. Without runtime validation, an attacker-controlled input or base can cause the resolved result to point to internal IPs, loopback, or arbitrary external hosts (SSRF), or to an attacker-controlled location when used in redirects (open redirect). The .d.ts itself contains no enforcement — it only documents the function signature, so the runtime implementation must perform these checks.
reviews.mitigation: After calling resolve(), parse the resulting string with a robust URL parser (e.g., platform URL API) and validate the canonicalized components. Enforce a scheme allow-list (e.g., http, https), enforce a host/origin allow-list for redirects or outgoing requests, and block private/loopback IPs or disallowed ports. Apply network egress restrictions for server-side requests and never directly trust the resolved string for redirects or outgoing connections.

reviews.issue: Resolved URI may contain unsafe schemes (javascript:, data:, file:) leading to XSS or local resource access when embedded into HTML or navigated to.
reviews.reasoning: The function signature accepts arbitrary strings and RELEVANT_CONTEXT notes the function does not restrict schemes. If callers insert the returned URI into HTML attributes (href/src) or use it for navigation without validating the scheme, dangerous schemes like javascript: or data: can enable XSS or unintended resource access. Because the declaration imposes no runtime constraints, callers must treat the resolved output as untrusted.
reviews.mitigation: Canonicalize and then enforce a safe scheme allow-list (e.g., only http and https) before embedding into HTML attributes or performing navigations. When inserting into HTML, properly escape/encode attribute values (or use safe APIs that set href/src) and avoid inserting unvalidated URIs directly into HTML.

reviews.issue: Resolved paths may enable path traversal when mapped to filesystem paths.
reviews.reasoning: RELEVANT_CONTEXT explains that resolve performs dot-segment removal and normalization and that some consumers may map URL paths to filesystem paths. If the resolved URI is canonicalized and then used to access the filesystem without constraining to a safe root, specially crafted relative inputs can escape intended directories (path traversal). The declaration itself does not prevent such misuse at runtime.
reviews.mitigation: If mapping a resolved URI to a filesystem path, canonicalize (realpath) and ensure the resulting path is within an expected directory (use a strict root allowlist). Reject or normalize inputs that resolve outside the allowed root. Prefer not to map arbitrary user-controlled URIs directly to filesystem locations.

reviews.issue: Potential for prototype pollution via object-assignment helpers referenced in the module surface.
reviews.reasoning: RELEVANT_CONTEXT lists helper operations such as assign(target, source) and notes that property copying from untrusted objects can be a vector for prototype pollution if the implementation does not defensively copy or restrict keys (e.g., allowing '__proto__' or 'constructor.prototype'). While the .d.ts does not show these helpers directly in this snippet, the context documents their presence on the module surface; thus the runtime implementation could expose an assign-like utility that, if used with untrusted input, risks prototype pollution.
reviews.mitigation: Ensure any runtime assign/merge utilities defensively validate keys against a whitelist before copying. Explicitly reject dangerous keys like '__proto__', 'constructor', or 'prototype'. Use safe copy patterns (Object.assign with guarded keys or create null-prototype targets) and prefer immutability for shared/global objects.

reviews.issue: Regex-based parsing or helpers (e.g., subexp) could be susceptible to ReDoS on crafted inputs.
reviews.reasoning: RELEVANT_CONTEXT mentions helpers like subexp(str) and implies regex-related transforms. Complex or poorly-written regular expressions operating on attacker-controlled URI strings can exhibit catastrophic backtracking leading to ReDoS (CPU exhaustion). The declaration file itself is type-only, so this is a runtime risk that implementers must mitigate.
reviews.mitigation: Avoid pathological or exponential-time regular expressions on untrusted inputs. Limit input length, use linear-time parsers where possible, and consider timeouts or work-queue limits for expensive operations. Fuzz/benchmark the implementation with large and malicious inputs to identify ReDoS patterns.

reviews.issue: Unchecked stack.pop() usage can throw on malformed input
reviews.reasoning: The decoder pops from the `stack` and immediately dereferences the result with a non-null assertion (`!`). If the input is malformed (e.g., contains a scope-closing marker without a matching open scope), `stack.pop()` will return `undefined` and the subsequent property assignments will throw a runtime TypeError. This is a denial-of-service/crash vector because the function accepts an externally-controlled string and does not validate stack state before popping. The same pattern also appears in decodeGeneratedRanges where `stack.pop()!` is used and then items are mutated.
reviews.mitigation: Check the return value of `stack.pop()` before using it. If it's `undefined`, handle the malformed input gracefully by returning a controlled parse error or throwing a descriptive exception. Example: `const last = stack.pop(); if (!last) { throw new Error('Malformed scopes: unmatched scope close'); } last[2] = line; ...` Avoid using non-null assertions for values derived from untrusted input.

reviews.issue: Unbounded parsing loops and allocations allow resource exhaustion (DoS)
reviews.reasoning: The decode functions iterate and allocate scope objects and arrays based entirely on the length and contents of an externally-supplied string. A maliciously large or crafted input can cause the decoder to allocate many objects (scopes, vars arrays, bindings) and perform expensive CPU work, potentially exhausting memory or CPU and causing denial-of-service in processes that call this function synchronously. There are no bounds checks on the number of scopes, number of vars per scope, or overall work performed.
reviews.mitigation: Enforce limits on input size and on the number of produced items. For example: set a maximum input length, maximum scopes parsed, maximum vars per scope, and maximum nesting depth. Fail fast with a clear parse error if limits are exceeded. Consider streaming/iterative parsing or performing decoding on a worker thread/process with resource caps for untrusted input.

reviews.issue: Shared mutable EMPTY array assigned to multiple scope.vars can cause cross-scope mutation
reviews.reasoning: When a scope has no vars the code assigns the same `EMPTY` array instance to `scope.vars`. If any consumer of the decoded scopes mutates `scope.vars` in-place, that mutation will affect all scopes that share the sentinel `EMPTY` array, causing cross-scope contamination and surprising behavior. While this is not a direct code injection, it is an integrity issue that may lead to incorrect behavior in downstream code and potential security consequences if downstream code assumes per-scope isolation.
reviews.mitigation: Avoid sharing a mutable sentinel array. Use an immutable empty array (e.g., `Object.freeze([])`) or allocate a fresh empty array per scope (`scope.vars = []`) even when no vars are present. Document whether `scope.vars` is expected to be mutated by callers and, if mutation is required, always provide unique arrays.

reviews.issue: Decoded integers are used directly as indices/identifiers without validation
reviews.reasoning: Integers decoded from the input are placed directly into arrays/tuples as source or scope indices. If downstream code uses these indices to index into arrays such as `sources` or `scopes` without bounds checking, a malicious input could produce out-of-range indices, leading to runtime errors or logic errors. The file accepts fully-controlled input and does not perform validation that indices are within expected bounds or non-negative where required.
reviews.mitigation: Validate decoded indices before using them as array indices or identifiers. Ensure values are non-negative integers, and optionally check against expected maximums (e.g., the length of `sources`/`names` arrays supplied by the caller). On invalid values, return a parse error instead of constructing ranges with invalid indices.

reviews.issue: Parsing loops rely on VLQ helpers for progress and may loop indefinitely on malformed input
reviews.reasoning: The code uses `hasMoreVlq` to determine whether to continue inner `do...while` loops that call `decodeInteger`. If `hasMoreVlq` or `decodeInteger` behaves incorrectly (for example, due to a bug or crafted input that keeps the reader position unchanged), these loops may never advance `reader.pos` and thus may not terminate, causing CPU exhaustion. Since the input is untrusted, the code should not assume that VLQ helper functions will always make progress and should defend against pathological cases.
reviews.mitigation: Add explicit iteration caps (e.g., maximum entries per loop) and sanity checks that the reader position has advanced on each decode iteration. If no progress is made after a reasonable number of iterations, abort with a parse error. Also review and harden `decodeInteger`/`hasMoreVlq` implementations to guarantee progress or to fail on malformed encodings.

reviews.issue: No input validation for 'encode' causing runtime exceptions or incorrect behavior on malformed segment arrays
reviews.reasoning: The encode function directly indexes into each 'segment' (e.g., segment[0], segment[1], etc.) and relies on segment.length to decide which indices exist. There is no runtime validation that 'decoded' is an array of arrays of numbers, that segment elements are numeric, or that a segment's length is exactly one of the expected shapes (1, 4, 5). If a caller passes malformed segments (wrong lengths, non-number values, NaN, Infinity, negative numbers) this can lead to runtime exceptions inside encodeInteger, generate invalid output, or produce unexpected deltas. The RELEVANT_CONTEXT explicitly notes that inputs are externally controlled and should be validated.
reviews.mitigation: Validate the 'decoded' structure at the API boundary before encoding: assert each line is an array, each segment is an array, and segment lengths are one of the expected sizes (1, 4, 5). Ensure all values are finite numbers within expected ranges (non-negative where appropriate). Fail-fast with a clear error if validation fails. Consider using TypeScript runtime type guards or a lightweight schema validator for untrusted inputs.

reviews.issue: Unbounded input size can lead to resource exhaustion (Denial of Service)
reviews.reasoning: Both decode and encode allocate data proportional to the size of the mappings: decode builds an array-of-lines and segments, and encode walks the entire 'decoded' structure and writes to an in-memory StringWriter. If an attacker supplies an extremely large mappings string or a decoded array with millions of segments/lines, the process can consume large amounts of memory and CPU, blocking the event loop and causing a DoS. The RELEVANT_CONTEXT explicitly calls out resource exhaustion as a risk.
reviews.mitigation: Enforce size limits on inputs from untrusted sources (e.g., max mappings string length, max number of lines, max segments per line). For large inputs, consider streaming/incremental parsing or processing with timeouts and backpressure. Fail with a controlled error if thresholds are exceeded.

reviews.issue: Trusting imported implementations (encodeInteger, StringWriter, StringReader) without integrity checks increases supply-chain risk
reviews.code_snippet:
import { comma, decodeInteger, encodeInteger, hasMoreVlq, semicolon } from './vlq';
import { StringWriter, StringReader } from './strings';
reviews.reasoning: The module's correctness and security depend critically on the imported functions/types (encodeInteger, decodeInteger, hasMoreVlq, StringWriter, StringReader). If these modules are modified or tampered with (supply-chain compromise, local file replacement), they could produce malformed output, throw exceptions, or introduce other malicious behavior. The RELEVANT_CONTEXT also highlights that the module's security is influenced by the implementations of these imports.
reviews.mitigation: Protect dependency integrity: use package-lock or yarn.lock with strict verification, enable package integrity checks (e.g., npm ci with lockfile), and consider reproducible builds or signing for critical dependencies. At runtime, consider defensive checks of outputs from imported functions (e.g., verify encodeInteger returns expected types/values) if operating on untrusted inputs.

reviews.issue: Decoded indices (sourceIndex, nameIndex) are not validated and may cause out-of-bounds access when used downstream
reviews.reasoning: The decode function produces numeric indices (sourcesIndex, namesIndex) that are intended to index into external 'sources' and 'names' arrays. There is no validation here that these indices are within the expected bounds (non-negative and less than the length of the corresponding arrays). If consumers later use these values directly to index into arrays provided separately, it can cause runtime exceptions or logic errors. The RELEVANT_CONTEXT warns that decoded numeric indices may be out of range and must be validated before use.
reviews.mitigation: Always validate decoded indices before using them to access external arrays: check non-negativity and upper bounds against the corresponding 'sources'/'names' arrays. Consider attaching metadata or performing a mapping validation step where mappings and source/name arrays are combined.

reviews.issue: No validation of low/high index parameters in binarySearch leading to potential out-of-bounds access and runtime exceptions (Denial of Service).
reviews.reasoning: binarySearch accepts caller-supplied low and high index arguments but does not validate them. If low or high are out of bounds (e.g., negative, or high >= haystack.length, or low > haystack.length-1) then the computed mid may be invalid and the expression haystack[mid][COLUMN] will attempt to access a non-existent element or an undefined value. Accessing haystack[mid] when mid is out-of-range or when haystack[mid] is undefined and then indexing [COLUMN] will throw a runtime TypeError, which can crash the host process or be used to trigger denial-of-service conditions. Since haystack and the index parameters may be derived from external source maps or user inputs, this is an exploitable robustness/availability issue.
reviews.mitigation: Validate and clamp low and high before using them. For example, ensure low is at least 0 and high is at most haystack.length - 1. Return a safe sentinel value or throw a well-documented error if caller-provided indices are invalid. Example checks at function start: if (!Array.isArray(haystack)) throw; low = Math.max(0, low|0); high = Math.min(haystack.length - 1, high|0); if (low > high) { found = false; return -1; } This prevents out-of-bounds mid values and avoids runtime exceptions.

reviews.issue: Exported mutable global 'found' variable allows external tampering and unreliable shared state across calls.
reviews.reasoning: The module exports a mutable top-level variable found which is set by binarySearch and memoizedBinarySearch. Because this is module-global and writable by any consumer that imports it, external code can read or overwrite it, causing callers to observe incorrect search results or to inadvertently influence control flow. In environments with concurrent asynchronous usage patterns (e.g., multiple interleaved calls in the same event loop), reliance on a single mutable global flag can produce race-like behavior: one call's result may be overwritten by another. This undermines correctness and can lead to logic errors in higher-level code that relies on the found flag.
reviews.mitigation: Avoid exporting mutable global state. Instead return an explicit result object from search functions (e.g., { index, found }) so each call's result is self-contained. If for backward compatibility you must keep the export, make it read-only or document that callers should not rely on it and provide a non-global API alternative. Example: change binarySearch to return an object or tuple and update callers.

reviews.issue: Direct property access on haystack elements without structural validation can invoke getters or crash when elements are malformed.
reviews.reasoning: The code assumes haystack is an array of simple arrays/tuples and accesses haystack[*][COLUMN] directly. If haystack is attacker-controlled or contains objects with getters for the COLUMN property or a prototype-modified structure, accessing that property can run arbitrary getter code in the same process, possibly causing side effects or exceptions. Also, if an element is undefined or not indexable, the direct access will throw. Since the haystack array can originate from decoded source maps or external data, it's safer to validate element shape before indexing.
reviews.mitigation: Validate that haystack is an array and that each accessed element is an array-like object with the expected numeric column value before using it. For example, cache const entry = haystack[mid]; if (!entry || typeof entry[COLUMN] !== 'number') { handle error/skip; } This prevents accidental invocation of getters and protects against malformed data causing exceptions. If performance is critical, validate inputs at the boundaries (once during parsing/decoding) so inner-loop access remains fast.

reviews.issue: Invalid or non-ASCII characters are not validated during decoding; charToInt lookup can silently map out-of-range character codes to 0.
reviews.reasoning: The charToInt lookup table is sized to 128 entries (covers ASCII codes 0..127). If reader.next() returns a character code >127 (e.g., non-ASCII/Unicode code points or a malformed input), indexing charToInt[c] will read an entry that was never initialized for that code. The implementation treats the result as a numeric integer without validation. This silently converts unexpected characters into numeric values (likely 0), causing incorrect decoding rather than an explicit failure. Because callers may feed untrusted source-map strings, malformed characters should be detected and rejected, not silently mapped.
reviews.mitigation: Validate that each character read from reader.next() is a member of the expected 64-character alphabet before using the derived integer. Concretely: check that c < charToInt.length and that the mapped value corresponds to an initialized mapping (e.g., maintain a sentinel value for unmapped entries or initialize charToInt with 0xFF and test for it). If an invalid character is encountered, throw or return an error instead of continuing decoding.

reviews.issue: Unbounded continuation loop in decodeInteger can be used for CPU exhaustion (denial-of-service) with crafted input.
reviews.reasoning: The decode loop continues while the continuation bit (integer & 32) is set. If an attacker supplies a long run of characters whose mapped integer always has the continuation bit set, the loop will continue until a terminating byte appears. Without an explicit iteration limit or boundary check inside decodeInteger, a malicious or malformed input can cause excessive CPU usage (and possibly trigger prolonged blocking of the event loop in Node.js or the UI thread in browsers). The function does not consult any caller-provided max length inside the loop, so responsibility is delegated entirely to the reader implementation and the caller.
reviews.mitigation: Introduce conservative iteration bounds or a max-byte/byte-count parameter into decodeInteger to stop decoding after a sensible number of continuation bytes (e.g., limit to the number of 5-bit groups needed to represent a 32-bit signed integer). If the limit is reached, throw a parse error. Also ensure the StringReader implementation enforces read bounds and reliably signals EOF instead of supplying repeating continuation bytes.

reviews.issue: hasMoreVlq trusts caller-provided 'max' boundary and reader.pos without validating them, which can lead to mis-detection of segment boundaries.
reviews.reasoning: hasMoreVlq uses reader.pos and a caller-provided max value to decide whether more VLQ values are present. If the caller supplies an incorrect max (too large) or if reader.pos is not reliably maintained by the reader (e.g., a custom reader that misreports position), the function can incorrectly report that more VLQ entries exist, potentially causing callers to read past intended boundaries. This is primarily a trust-boundary issue: the function assumes 'max' and reader.pos are correct and does not validate them.
reviews.mitigation: Document and enforce the contract for max and reader.pos or, better, accept a reader method that reports remaining bytes or a reliable length instead of trusting external numeric max. Validate that max is within the reader's buffer length and clamp/guard reader accesses accordingly. Consider having hasMoreVlq accept an explicit end-index from the same source that built the reader so both come from the same trusted origin.

reviews.issue: encodeInteger writes numeric char codes to builder.write without guaranteeing the writer's expected input type.
reviews.reasoning: The code calls builder.write(intToChar[clamped]) where intToChar is a Uint8Array of ASCII codes. This passes a numeric byte value to builder.write. Whether this produces the intended character depends on the StringWriter implementation: some writers may expect a string, some may accept a numeric code, and some may treat numbers differently (e.g., coerce to string representation of the number). If the writer expects strings, the generated mappings will be malformed. While not strictly a security vulnerability by itself, wrong output could later cause parsing errors or unexpected behavior when that output is consumed by other components.
reviews.mitigation: Ensure an explicit and documented contract for builder.write: either accept and document numeric char codes, or convert the char code to a single-character string before writing (e.g., builder.write(String.fromCharCode(intToChar[clamped]))). Validate the writer implementation used in your environment to avoid accidental mismatches.

reviews.issue: Malformed or unexpected input is not explicitly reported; decodeInteger returns numeric results for corrupted inputs instead of throwing errors.
reviews.reasoning: When decodeInteger encounters malformed characters or unexpected values, it continues computation using the numeric mapping produced by charToInt without throwing. As a result, corrupted or out-of-spec mappings produce numeric results that may be silently accepted by callers. Downstream code using these decoded integers (e.g., as array indices, sizes, or loop bounds) could then behave incorrectly — potentially causing out-of-bounds access, excessive allocations, or logic errors. There's no explicit error path indicating malformed input.
reviews.mitigation: Fail fast on malformed input: if a character is not part of the base64 VLQ alphabet or if the decoded value exceeds reasonable bounds, throw a descriptive parse error. Add validation after decoding to ensure returned values are within expected ranges before they are used to index arrays or allocate resources.

reviews.issue: Unvalidated construction of resolved URIs from external inputs (sourceRoot, source, mapUrl)
reviews.reasoning: The resolver concatenates an optional sourceRoot (prefix) with the provided source and passes that as the first argument to resolveUri, while using stripFilename(mapUrl) as the base. The function parameters mapUrl, sourceRoot, and the returned function's source parameter are all externally-supplied inputs in typical usage (they come from sourcemaps and caller code). There is no validation or sanitization in this file of the concatenated result or the base. As noted in the provided RELEVANT_CONTEXT, resolved URIs are later used by trace-mapping consumers for lookups, content retrieval, or comparisons. If downstream code performs filesystem access or network requests using these resolved values, attackers controlling the sourcemap or inputs could cause path traversal/local-file access (e.g., via ../ sequences or file:// URIs) or induce network requests to attacker-controlled hosts (SSRF). The code in this file is the point where untrusted strings are combined into a final URI without checks, which is the immediate source of those risks.
reviews.mitigation: Validate and canonically normalize the resolved result before it is used by downstream code. Specific mitigations: (1) Enforce an allowlist of permissible URI schemes (e.g., only http/https if network fetches are intended, or explicitly allow file: only behind strict guardrails). (2) After resolution, verify that the resulting path/URI lies within an expected root directory when used for filesystem access (reject or normalize paths that escape the intended root). (3) Reject or sanitize protocol-relative or dangerous schemes (data:, file:, ftp:) unless explicitly required and authorized. (4) Limit input sizes and add unit tests for edge cases (long paths, .. sequences, percent-encoding). (5) Add logging/auditing of resolved URIs and their origins so usage can be reviewed.

reviews.issue: Reliance on third-party resolve-uri behavior for normalization and scheme handling without post-validation
reviews.reasoning: The module delegates URI/path resolution and normalization to an external library (@jridgewell/resolve-uri). Any bugs, unexpected normalization behavior, or changes in that dependency (for example how it treats protocol-relative URIs, percent-encoding, or scheme preservation) can affect the final resolved values produced by this resolver. Because this file does not apply any post-resolution validation (it directly returns resolveUri's result), the behavior and security characteristics of the resolver depend on the external package's correctness and its version. The RELEVANT_CONTEXT explicitly calls out that the resolver depends on the URI resolution implementation and that such dependencies can affect security posture.
reviews.mitigation: Pin and audit the resolve-uri dependency version used in your project and review its changelog for behavior changes. Add comprehensive tests for edge cases (protocol-relative URLs, file:, data:, percent-encoding, and path traversal cases) to ensure the dependency behaves as expected. Crucially, perform post-resolution validation in this module (e.g., check scheme allowlist, enforce path containment rules) so behavior is not solely delegated to the third-party library.

reviews.issue: Unvalidated sourceIndex/sourceLine access can cause runtime exceptions (Denial of Service)
reviews.reasoning: The code indexes into the sources array using values read from seg (sourceIndex, sourceLine) without validating their type or range. If sourceIndex is out of range (e.g. >= sources.length) or not a valid index, sources[sourceIndex] will be undefined and attempting to access source[sourceLine] will throw a TypeError. Because this function processes caller-supplied decoded mapping arrays, a crafted or malformed source map can trigger an exception and crash or terminate processing, leading to a denial-of-service (DoS) condition in callers that do not catch the error.
reviews.mitigation: Validate and sanitize indices before using them. For example, coerce indices to integers and check: typeof sourceIndex === 'number' && Number.isFinite(sourceIndex) && sourceIndex >= 0 && sourceIndex < sources.length. Likewise validate sourceLine as a non-negative integer and that source (sources[sourceIndex]) is defined. If an index is invalid, skip the segment or handle it gracefully (e.g. continue). Wrap processing in try/catch at appropriate call boundaries if untrusted input may be present.

reviews.issue: Prototype-pollution/vector property mutation via untrusted indices (setting __proto__ or other special keys)
reviews.reasoning: Arrays in JavaScript are objects; indexing with an unvalidated key can set non-numeric properties such as "__proto__" or other special property names. If seg[SOURCE_LINE] or seg[SOURCES_INDEX] are attacker-controlled and contain "__proto__" (or other prototype-special keys), the ||= assignment may modify the prototype chain or other unexpected properties on the array/object, leading to prototype pollution. Prototype pollution can lead to wide-ranging invariant breaks, unexpected behavior, or elevations of privilege in code that later relies on these data structures.
reviews.mitigation: Avoid using plain arrays/objects with untrusted keys. Coerce and validate keys to numeric indices before using them (e.g. const lineIndex = Number(sourceLine); if (!Number.isInteger(lineIndex) || lineIndex < 0) continue). Alternatively use a Map<number, ...> for mappings keyed by untrusted values, or create objects with no prototype (Object.create(null)) and restrict keys. Explicitly reject or sanitize any keys like "__proto__", "constructor", "prototype".

reviews.issue: Unbounded memory/CPU usage when processing large/ malicious decoded mapping arrays (resource exhaustion)
reviews.reasoning: The function builds in-memory arrays for every entry in memos and then pushes one tuple per mapping segment. If an attacker supplies a very large decoded mapping array (or very many segments per line), this will allocate large arrays and perform potentially expensive sorts for each source line, causing high memory usage and CPU consumption. In an environment where source maps are accepted from untrusted clients (uploads, network), this can be exploited to cause denial-of-service by exhausting memory or blocking the event loop during sort operations.
reviews.mitigation: Enforce limits on input sizes: bound decoded.length, the number of segments per line, and memos.length before allocating arrays. Consider streaming/incremental processing instead of materializing all mappings in memory at once. Skip or truncate sources that exceed thresholds, or throw a controlled error. When sorting, avoid sorting extremely large arrays in a single synchronous operation—use chunking or offload to worker threads if necessary.

reviews.issue: Preserves sourcesContent and returns full source content in flattened map, risking information disclosure.
reviews.reasoning: The implementation collects sourcesContent from input maps and includes the entire sourcesContent array verbatim in the resulting flattened DecodedSourceMap (joined). Since sourcesContent frequently contains original source code, returning or exposing this flattened map can leak sensitive application source or internal file paths to any consumer of the flattened map. The input maps are externally controlled and should be treated as untrusted — including full sourcesContent in outputs creates an information-disclosure risk when maps are stored, served, or logged.
reviews.mitigation: Make inclusion of sourcesContent configurable (e.g., a flag to strip or redact sourcesContent). Provide an option to omit sourcesContent by default when processing untrusted input. Enforce size and content limits on sourcesContent (max length per source and total size). Document the sensitivity and warn callers that sourcesContent contains raw source code.

reviews.issue: No validation of mapping segment source/name indexes before using them; may cause runtime exceptions or incorrect mapping indices.
reviews.reasoning: The code indexes into the segment array (seg) using constants like SOURCES_INDEX and NAMES_INDEX and then uses those values to compute indexes into merged arrays (sourcesOffset + seg[SOURCES_INDEX]). There are no checks that seg has the expected length, that seg[SOURCES_INDEX] or seg[NAMES_INDEX] are numbers, or that computed indexes fall within the bounds of the corresponding source/name arrays. Malformed or adversarially-crafted mappings could contain missing/invalid indices or indices out of range, which can lead to undefined values, incorrect flattened mappings, or runtime exceptions that crash the process.
reviews.mitigation: Validate each segment before use: check seg.length, ensure seg[SOURCES_INDEX] and seg[NAMES_INDEX] (when present) are integers within expected ranges (e.g., 0 <= index < map.resolvedSources.length or map.names.length). On invalid segments, either skip the segment, clamp to safe defaults, or return a controlled error. Wrap critical parsing in try/catch and return descriptive errors rather than allowing unhandled exceptions.

reviews.issue: Unbounded growth of the mappings array via getLine can lead to large memory allocations (DoS) if mapping line indices or section offsets are large.
reviews.reasoning: getLine expands the mappings array by creating empty arrays up to the requested index. If lineI (derived from lineOffset + i) is very large (e.g., controlled by an attacker-supplied section offset or malicious map), the loop will allocate many empty arrays, consuming large amounts of memory and potentially crashing or degrading the host process. There is no bounding, quota, or validation on offsets/indices before this allocation, so malicious inputs can cause resource exhaustion.
reviews.mitigation: Enforce limits on maximum allowed line indices and total lines/segments processed (e.g., a configurable MAX_LINES). Validate section offsets and abort processing if resulting indexes exceed thresholds. Consider using sparse data structures (e.g., Map keyed by line number) instead of pre-allocating arrays, or incrementally stream processing so a single huge index cannot cause O(index) allocations.

reviews.issue: Bypassing TypeScript type safety by forwarding 'arguments' with a cast when recursively calling recurse.
reviews.code_snippet: if ('sections' in parsed) return recurse(...(arguments as unknown as Parameters<typeof recurse>));
reviews.reasoning: This line uses the runtime 'arguments' object and casts it to the typed parameter list of recurse. That bypasses TypeScript's compile-time checks and may forward unexpected or incorrectly-typed values to recurse at runtime. Such type-safety bypasses can hide bugs and make it easier for malformed or attacker-controlled inputs to flow into internal APIs unchecked, increasing the risk that a validation omission becomes exploitable.
reviews.mitigation: Avoid forwarding the raw arguments object with a type cast. Instead, explicitly call recurse with well-typed, validated arguments (e.g., recurse(parsed, mapUrl, mappings, sources, sourcesContent, names, ignoreList, lineOffset, columnOffset, stopLine, stopColumn)). Where necessary, construct a properly validated parameter array before forwarding.

reviews.issue: No input validation for segment/mapping structure; indexing can throw or produce NaN during sort
reviews.reasoning: The code assumes each entry in mappings (and each segment in a line) is an array with a numeric value at index COLUMN. isSorted directly indexes line[j][COLUMN] and sortComparator subtracts a[COLUMN] - b[COLUMN]. If a segment is not an array, missing the index, or contains non-numeric values (undefined, string, object), these operations will produce undefined/NaN or throw a TypeError, causing unpredictable sort results or uncaught exceptions during initialization. Since mappings are external input (source maps can be provided from files or network), malformed or malicious maps can cause the library to crash or behave incorrectly.
reviews.mitigation: Validate and sanitize the mappings before sorting: ensure each mapping line is an array and each segment is an array with a numeric COLUMN value. Coerce values to Number safely (e.g., Number(value) and check Number.isFinite) or reject malformed segments with a clear error. Add defensive guards before indexing (e.g., if (!Array.isArray(line[j]) || !Number.isFinite(line[j][COLUMN])) { handle error/skip }). Consider wrapping comparator calls in try/catch or using a safe comparator that treats missing/invalid columns deterministically.

reviews.issue: No resource limits or safeguards against very large mapping arrays — possible CPU/Memory exhaustion (DoS)
reviews.reasoning: The function can allocate copies of the top-level array and per-line arrays, then call JavaScript's native sort on each line. There are no checks on the total number of lines, number of segments per line, or overall size of the mappings. An attacker supplying extremely large source maps (many lines or very large per-line segment arrays) can cause high CPU usage and memory allocation during slice() and sort(), leading to application slowdown or crash (Denial of Service).
